docs.title: An ASIC-bound NPU compute tile, specified end to end
docs.text:
mininpu · compute-tile documentation

A composable NPU IP template (MXU / VPU / tile / NoC) built as an IO-attached discrete accelerator. Three documents: a locked instruction-set reference plus two work-in-progress design docs. FPGA is a test platform only; the design stays ASIC-targeting.

  • ISA 0.5.0 · 15 opcodes
  • geometry N=8
  • VREG 32 x [8x8] fp32
  • on-chip SPAD 32 KiB
  • target GPT-2

docs.title: 01 Document set
docs.text: The compute-tile design in three parts. Doc 1 is frozen; Docs 2 and 3 are being authored.

docs.title: Doc 1 ISA 0.5.0 Specification (Locked)
docs.text: The complete instruction-set reference: 15 opcodes, 64-bit fixed word, in-order single issue, no hardware interlock, and the full compiler scheduling contract.

docs.text: The datapath behind the ISA: concurrency model, per-unit AGU, MXU / VPU / VREG / ACC / SPAD, and the MXU-VPU software pipeline. Locked design plus open decisions.

docs.title: Doc 3 Implementation (Files & Modules) (In progress)
docs.text: How the design maps to RTL: the tile-sequencer fold, DMU internals, and the target file/module hierarchy. Locked decisions plus pending proposals.

docs.title: DMU: Data Moving Unit - Design Brief
docs.text:
mininpu · Doc 4 · Design brief

Consolidated, doc-first design brief for the compute-tile async DMA unit. It assembles already-locked decisions and the existing DMA RTL surface into one authoritative contract; it does not invent new microarchitecture. Everything is split into LOCKED (the contract the DMU must honor) and OPEN (the implementer's design space).

  • compute-tile DMU
  • async, 2 slots
  • 0x40 load · 0x42 store
  • v1 single-outstanding
  • SPAD only, no ACC

docs.title: Table of contents
docs.text:
• 00 How to read this brief
  • 01 Role & Doctrine
  • 02 ISA Surface
  • 03 Data Path & Memory
  • 04 Existing-RTL Surface
  • 05 Interfaces the DMU Exposes
  • 06 v1 Scope vs Deferred
  • 07 OPEN Design Space
  • 08 Block Diagram Sketch
  • 09 For the Implementing Session

docs.title: 00 How to read this brief
docs.text:
Two buckets, marked on every claim. Design inside OPEN; do not re-open LOCKED.

Locked

Contract the DMU must honor. Fixed by the charter (SSOT), the ISA SSOT, and the existing RTL surface already on main. Changing any of these is out of scope for the implementing session; it would ripple into the ISA, the ABI, or the compiler contract.

Open

The implementer designs this. Per session-decision §M the DMU internals (burst sizing, FIFO / landing-buffer depth, dm_axi error/timeout policy, slot FSM, the precise SPAD arbitration handshake, and the exact descriptor encoding) are deliberately deferred to RTL implementation. Every OPEN item below lists the constraints it must satisfy, so the choice is bounded, not blank.

Source anchors are cited as file:line against the tree at origin/main. Primary SSOT: charter §4-§5 (docs/charter.md), ISA (mininpu/isa/opcodes.py, mininpu/isa/encoders.py), hardware parameters (mininpu/hwparams.py), and the DMA RTL under npu/src/.

docs.title: 01 Role & Doctrine
docs.text:
Where the DMU sits and the async model it implements.

Locked · placement

The DMU lives in the compute tile, following the Gemmini LoadController model. The interface tile stays devmem-only: it owns the AXI path to off-chip memory, not the SPAD-fill orchestration. Charter §5: "The DMA unit lives in the compute tile (Gemmini LoadController model); the interface tile stays devmem-only." The sequencer keeps the DMU external to itself: tile_sequencer exposes dma_slot_done / dmu_idle stubs and dmu.sv is a separate module (session-decision §J).

Locked · async dual-port ping-pong

The DMU is async-only (session-decision §A; ISA Doc 1 §4). An async DMA request runs in the background, filling SPAD while the sequencer computes on the compute-side SPAD port (dual-port ping-pong). Charter §5: "an async DMA request (descriptor flag) runs in the background, filling SPAD port A while the sequencer computes on port B." The compute pipeline never stalls for the DMU except at an explicit flush.slot fence: the DMU absorbs all SPAD arbitration variability by yielding (session-decision §I).

Locked · completion token

Completion is signalled by dma_slot_done[slot], the VTA-style dependence token. flush.slot is the fence that waits on it. Charter §5 / §4: "Completion = dma_slot_done[slot] (the VTA-style dependence token); flush.slot fences" and "async DMA + dma_slot_done IS in v1 ... must be RTL-verified."

docs.title: Sync vs async
docs.text:
• Async is the only DMU mode in v1. The former async-mode flag (flags[5] on the DMA opcodes) is retired and is now reserved-must-be-zero (ISA Doc 1 §4; session-decision §A). There is no synchronous DMU opcode variant.
  • The host-facing synchronous block transfers (host<->devmem memcpy) are a separate concern, handled by the command processor + copy engine over the AXI-Lite CMD window (charter §4), not by the compute-tile DMU.

docs.title: 02 ISA Surface
docs.text:
The two DMU opcodes, their operand slots, the fence, and the slot count. All LOCKED (ISA SSOT).

op opcode direction touches SSOT dmu.load.spm 0x40 DRAM (bf16) → SPAD SPAD only opcodes.py:505 dmu.store.spm 0x42 SPAD → DRAM (bf16) SPAD only opcodes.py:506 flush.slot 0x53 fence (masked stall) per-slot done bits opcodes.py:510 halt 0x5F terminal drain all slots opcodes.py:512

Locked · no ACC

The DMU never touches ACC. The dmu.*.acc opcodes are removed (no OP_DMU_*_ACC in the SSOT). ACC is MXU-owned fp32 and never reaches DRAM; async DMA targets the SPAD region only and flush.slot guards SPAD. opcodes.py:232: "The DMU touches SPAD only (ACC never reaches DRAM)."

docs.title: 64-bit encoding, operand slots
docs.text:
The instruction word is the fixed 64-bit format OP[63:56] | FLAGS[55:48] | op0[47:32] | op1[31:16] | op2[15:0] (ISA Doc 1 §3). The DMU opcodes use these slots (encoders.py:436-548):

fielddmu.load.spm (0x40)dmu.store.spm (0x42) op0SPAD dst rowSPAD src row op1literal-pool index of dram_addr (IMM_OP1)literal-pool index of dram_addr (IMM_OP1) op2(mat_rows-1)<<8 | (mat_cols-1), packed 7+7packed shape, or 0xFFFF "derive at lowering" sentinel pool adjacencydram_stride is force-appended at pool[op1+1] so the descriptor-lowering pass (W6) reads addr + stride contiguously flagsIMM_OP1 set (address is wide); dtype in base flags; flags[5] reserved-must-be-zero (retired async flag)
  • dram_stride = bytes-per-DRAM-row. Row r reads dram_addr + r*dram_stride. dram_stride=0 is a first-class broadcast mode on load (all dst rows filled from one DRAM row - this is how bias is added). encoders.py:467-490.
  • Inline form only; the W6 assembler.py pass lowers the inline op into a descriptor + trigger. The exact descriptor format the DMU consumes is OPEN (see §07).

Locked · slots & fence

DMA_SLOT_COUNT = 2 independent async slots (hwparams.py:80). flush.slot takes a 16-bit slot bitmask in op0: bit i set = wait for slot i to drain; flush.slot 0xFFFF waits for every slot. It is the only fence primitive (the unmasked flush 0x52 is retired). halt drains all outstanding slots then asserts done. encoders.py:75-88.

docs.title: 03 Data Path & Memory
docs.text:
devmem, the DM word, the AXI bridge, the bandwidth story, and SPAD arbitration priority. All LOCKED.

fact value SSOT device memory off-chip PL-DDR4, reached via dm_axi_bridge → m00_axi charter §5 DM word width DM_DATA_WIDTH = 256 bits (8 × 32-bit), matching SPAD row width hwparams.py:63 DM address space DM_ADDR_WIDTH = 27 (256-bit words); byte addr = dm_addr × 32 hwparams.py:62 as-built AXI 32-bit AXI @ 50 MHz (~200 MB/s, ~1% of DDR peak) charter §5 SPAD 1024 × 256b = 32 KiB, 8 banks (row mod 8), bf16 hwparams.py:52,87 SPAD read latency MEM_LATENCY = 2 cycles (registered BRAM output) hwparams.py:66

Locked · bandwidth - bus width is the lever

The as-built dm_axi_bridge is 32-bit AXI @ 50 MHz. The bottleneck is bus width, not outstanding depth. At 32b/50 MHz the bandwidth-delay product is ~1 burst, so multiple-outstanding buys ~nothing until the AXI path is widened (toward the 256-bit DM-word width) and/or clocked up. Charter §6 notes the DDR/AXI clock is independent of the compute-tile clock (a CDC at the DMEM boundary lets the AXI side run at the MIG user clock, 200-300 MHz), which is the lever that makes the widened path real. This bounds v1 to single-outstanding (see §06).

Locked · SPAD arbitration priority

SPAD has a compute-side port and a DMU access path. On any structural conflict, compute has fixed priority and the DMU yields. Because the DMU is async and flush.slot-gated, yielding never stalls compute. Session-decision §I: "DMU ABSORBS arbitration variability (yields on conflict; it is async/flush-gated), so compute never stalls. Reuse the banked_sram arbiter, priority compute(fixed) > DMU(yields)." The precise handshake (dedicated port A fill vs. a lower-priority compute master on the banked_sram grant arbiter) is OPEN - see §04 and §07.

docs.title: 04 Existing-RTL Surface (reuse, do not rebuild)
docs.text:
Four modules already on main provide most of the mechanism. The DMU orchestrates them; it does not re-implement them.

modulekey ports (real)already provides dma_engine.svinterface_tile start, mode[3:0], slot_id[0:0], done; dma_slot_done[1:0] (sticky), clear_slot_done[1:0]; stream_ready; stream/BRAM control (data_write_en, instr_write_en, start_stream, read_en) 2-slot completion tracking. Per-slot sub-FSM (IDLE/ACTIVE/DONE), round-robin fair-share arbitration between slots, sticky dma_slot_done[i] asserted on completion and held until clear_slot_done[i]. dma_engine.sv:77-111,170-244 dm_axi_bridge.svinterface_tile dm_addr[26:0], dm_din[255:0], dm_dout[255:0], dm_en, dm_we, dm_ack  <->  full m_axi master (AR/R/AW/W/B) BRAM-style DM port → AXI4 burst. One DM word = one 8-beat INCR burst (arlen/awlen=7, arsize/awsize=2); byte addr = {dm_addr,5'b0}. Single-ID, single-outstanding, in-order. dm_ack = combinational 1-cycle pulse in DONE. The sole m00_axi master. dm_axi_bridge.sv:35-80,84-107 dma_addr_gen.svcompute_tile base_addr[31:0], row[7:0], stride[31:0] → byte_addr[31:0] Row byte-address generator (AGU). Pure combinational base_addr + row*stride (32-bit truncated). Already instantiated twice in the sequencer (foreground DMA row loop + the #222 background async-DMA track). dma_addr_gen.sv:18-25 spad.svcompute_tile Port A DMA side: dma_wr_en, dma_wr_data[255:0], dma_write_pointer, dma_rd_data[255:0], dma_read_pointer. Port B compute masters: dma_comp_*_b (port 0) + comp1_*_b (port 1), each with *_gnt_b 8-bank TDP scratchpad + a per-bank grant arbiter. True dual-port banked_sram: Port A DMA side, Port B carries N arbitrated compute masters with fixed priority port 0 > port 1 via comp_gnt_v / comp_conflict_bank_o (a lower-priority master advances only on a granted cycle). This grant mechanism is the "compute fixed > DMU yields" primitive. spad.sv:45-121,128-150

Note · RTL-vs-target framing

The as-built dma_engine.sv lives in the interface tile and decodes the host block-transfer modes (stream write/read, IRAM load); its 2-slot machinery and dma_slot_done are the completion mechanism the DMU reuses. The target DMU is a compute-tile module (dmu.sv) that drives dma_addr_gen + the dm_* bridge port and the SPAD DMA path. Reuse these ports and the 2-slot/grant primitives; do not rebuild them. How the slot machinery relocates or is re-instantiated inside the compute tile is an implementation choice (§07).

docs.title: 05 Interfaces the DMU Module Must Expose
docs.text: Shape is LOCKED; exact bit-encoding is OPEN. The DMU sits between the sequencer, SPAD, and the dm_* bridge port.

docs.title: 1. Sequencer descriptor-in
docs.text:
field meaning status base DRAM base byte address (row 0), from the literal pool LOCKED present SPAD row dst row (load) / src row (store) LOCKED present length (rows × cols) mat_rows, mat_cols (packed 7+7 in op2) LOCKED present stride bytes-per-DRAM-row; 0 = broadcast (load) LOCKED present direction load (0x40) vs store (0x42) LOCKED present slot which of the 2 async slots this transfer occupies LOCKED concept, OPEN encoding

The field set is fixed by the ISA (§02). The exact wire format of the descriptor the sequencer hands the DMU (bit packing, how slot is assigned/carried, how the pool-indexed base/stride are resolved to values) is OPEN (§07).

docs.title: 2. SPAD port + arbitration handshake
docs.text:
• LOCKED shape: a SPAD read/write access path (256-bit rows, MEM_LATENCY=2) with a yield-on-conflict handshake; compute has fixed priority.
  • OPEN detail: whether the DMU uses the dedicated Port A fill path or is wired as a lower-priority master on the Port B banked_sram grant arbiter, and the exact en/gnt/retry protocol (§07).

docs.title: 3. Completion out + clear
docs.text: • LOCKED: drive dma_slot_done[1:0] (sticky, one bit per slot) on transfer completion; accept a clear_slot_done[1:0] write-1-to-clear. This is exactly the dma_engine.sv contract (dma_engine.sv:87-89) and it must be RTL-verified in v1 (charter §4).

docs.title: 4. flush.slot handshake
docs.text:
• LOCKED: expose per-slot status so the sequencer can masked-stall on flush.slot (op0 bitmask) and drain all slots on halt. The sequencer already stubs dma_slot_done / dmu_idle for this.
  • OPEN: the exact busy/idle signalling and the stall-release timing contract (§07).

docs.title: 5. dm_* to the bridge
docs.text: • LOCKED: drive the dm_addr / dm_din / dm_dout / dm_en / dm_we / dm_ack BRAM-style port of dm_axi_bridge (dm_axi_bridge.sv:35-40); one DM word per handshake, dm_ack ends the transaction. The AGU byte address feeds dm_addr via its [DM_BYTE_SHIFT +: DM_ADDR_WIDTH] slice.

docs.title: 06 v1 Scope vs Deferred
docs.text:
What v1 must deliver, and what is explicitly out of v1.

Locked · v1 = make-async-real, single-outstanding

v1 makes the async path real and RTL-verified: dma.load.spm / dma.store.spm run in the background, complete via dma_slot_done[slot], and are fenced by flush.slot. v1 is single-outstanding: one transfer in flight hides the latency. The two slots (DMA_SLOT_COUNT=2) exist in the HW contract, but v1 does not exploit multiple-outstanding, because at 32b/50 MHz the BDP is ~1 burst (§03). Charter §5: "[v1] = make-async-real with single-outstanding (one slot in flight hides latency)."

Deferred · [v1+], out of v1

Multiple-outstanding + coalescing + a landing/reorder buffer are [v1+], layered only after the AXI path is widened toward the 256-bit DM word. The landing/reorder buffer is sized to the bandwidth-delay product (= target BW × DDR latency). Charter §5 / §13. These are explicitly not in v1 and the v1 DMU must not be blocked on them - but its slot/descriptor structure should not preclude them.

docs.title: 07 OPEN Design Space
docs.text:
The implementer's job (session-decision §M). Each item is bounded by the constraints it must satisfy - design within them.

OPEN itemconstraints it must satisfy Descriptor format / encoding Must carry the §05 field set (base, SPAD row, rows×cols, stride, direction, slot). Must be producible by the W6 assembler.py descriptor-lowering pass from the inline 0x40/0x42 form, reading dram_addr at pool[op1] and dram_stride at pool[op1+1]. Broadcast (stride=0) must be representable. Slot FSM 2 slots (DMA_SLOT_COUNT=2), each IDLE→ACTIVE→DONE. Must drive sticky dma_slot_done[i] until clear_slot_done[i]. Must support the flush.slot masked fence and full drain on halt. v1 single-outstanding, but the structure should not preclude [v1+] multiple-outstanding. May reuse the dma_engine.sv round-robin fairness. Burst sizing Bounded by the bridge: one DM word = one 8-beat INCR burst, 256-bit word, byte addr = dm_addr×32. Row loop iterates base + row*stride via dma_addr_gen. Must respect the single-outstanding, in-order, single-ID bridge (no reordering, one transaction to dm_ack). FIFO / landing-buffer depth v1 single-outstanding needs only enough buffering to bridge SPAD-side (MEM_LATENCY=2) and the AXI burst. A landing/reorder buffer sized to the BDP is [v1+] (after AXI widening). Do not oversize for v1; do not preclude the [v1+] buffer. dm_axi error / timeout policy The bridge accepts/ignores rresp/bresp today (single-ID, in-order). Define how the DMU handles a stalled/never-acked transaction and any error response. Must not silently corrupt SPAD or hang the flush.slot fence. Sim target is a clean cocotbext-axi AxiRam (charter §5), so the policy is about robustness, not a modeled fault. Precise SPAD arbitration handshake Compute fixed priority > DMU yields; compute never stalls; the DMU absorbs the variability (async / flush-gated). Reuse the banked_sram grant arbiter (comp_gnt / comp_conflict_bank_o) or the dedicated Port A path. Define the en/gnt/retry protocol and how a yielded DMU beat is re-driven. 256-bit rows, MEM_LATENCY=2.

docs.title: 08 Block Diagram Sketch
docs.text:
Structural layout (information + placement fixed here; aesthetic polish is a later session's job). Descriptor slots → AGU → burst/AXI engine → PL-DDR4, plus SPAD-port arbitration and the dma_slot_done / flush.slot token path.

SEQUENCER tile_sequencer issues 0x40 / 0x42 flush.slot fence (0x53) DMU (compute-tile, dmu.sv) Descriptor slots slot 0 / slot 1 base, SPAD row, len, stride, dir, slot Slot FSM (OPEN) IDLE-ACTIVE-DONE x2 round-robin fairness AGU dma_addr_gen base + row*stride (reused, combinational) Burst / AXI engine (OPEN) drives dm_* port: dm_addr / dm_din / dm_dout dm_en / dm_we / dm_ack FIFO / landing buf (OPEN) error / timeout (OPEN) v1: single-outstanding, 1 DM word = 8-beat burst INTERFACE TILE dm_axi_bridge 8-beat INCR burst sole m00_axi master PL-DDR4 devmem, bf16 32b @ 50 MHz (v1) COMPUTE-TILE SPAD spad.sv (8-bank TDP) Port B: compute (fixed prio) Port A / grant: DMU (yields) 256b rows, MEM_LATENCY=2 COMPUTE MXU / VPU / LSU computes on Port B m00_axi SPAD fill / drain dma_slot_done[slot] token → flush.slot clear_slot_done (W1C)
  • Green = existing/external RTL reused as-is (interface tile bridge, PL-DDR4, the SPAD TDP macro, the compute masters). Teal = the DMU the session builds; boxes marked (OPEN) are the design space.
  • Dashed amber = the dependence-token path: dma_slot_done[slot] back to the sequencer where flush.slot consumes it; clear_slot_done is write-1-to-clear.
  • The SPAD arrow is deliberately drawn as a single "fill / drain" path: whether it is the dedicated Port A or a yielding Port B master is OPEN (§07).

docs.title: 09 For the Implementing Session
docs.text: A short checklist: what to read, what is fixed, what to decide, what to test.

docs.title: Read first
docs.text:
• docs/charter.md §4-§5 (the SSOT doctrine).
  • mininpu/isa/opcodes.py + mininpu/isa/encoders.py (0x40 / 0x42 / 0x53 / 0x5F encodings).
  • The four RTL modules in §04: npu/src/interface_tile/dma_engine.sv, npu/src/interface_tile/dm_axi_bridge.sv, npu/src/compute_tile/dma_addr_gen.sv, npu/src/compute_tile/spad.sv (and npu/src/common/banked_sram.sv for the grant arbiter).
  • The tile_sequencer dma_slot_done / dmu_idle stubs (the DMU is kept external).

docs.title: Fixed (do not re-open)
docs.text:
• Placement (compute tile), async-only, SPAD-only (no ACC).
  • Opcodes 0x40 / 0x42, flush.slot fence, dma_slot_done[slot] completion, DMA_SLOT_COUNT=2.
  • devmem = PL-DDR4 via dm_axi_bridge→m00_axi; 256-bit DM word; compute-fixed > DMU-yields SPAD priority.
  • v1 = make-async-real, single-outstanding; multiple-outstanding/coalescing/landing-buffer are [v1+].

docs.title: Decide (the OPEN list, §07)
docs.text: • Exact descriptor format/encoding; slot FSM; burst sizing; FIFO / landing-buffer depth; dm_axi error/timeout policy; the precise SPAD arbitration handshake.

docs.title: Tests to write
docs.text:
• dma.load.spm round-trip: preload a cocotbext-axi AxiRam on m00_axi, run dmu.load.spm, assert SPAD rows match (including a stride=0 broadcast case and a multi-row strided case).
  • dma.store.spm round-trip: write SPAD, run dmu.store.spm, assert AxiRam contents match (bf16, row-major, strided).
  • dma_slot_done token: assert the sticky per-slot done bit asserts on completion and clears on clear_slot_done (write-1-to-clear); RTL-verified per charter §4.
  • flush.slot fence: a compute op reading a DMU-filled SPAD row after flush.slot <mask> must observe the completed data; before the fence it must not be relied upon. Verify masked (single-slot) and full (halt) drain.
  • Non-stall: with a DMU transfer in flight, compute Port B access proceeds every cycle (DMU yields on bank conflict).

Reminder

Per the repo DV gate, run the relevant simulation after any RTL change and (if EXEC_DISPATCH is touched) python3 dv/scripts/check_isa_coverage.py. The DMU opcodes already exist in the ISA SSOT, so this brief implies no ISA change - only new RTL + DV.

As-built, pre-merge

This page tracks the real tree on origin/main plus the feature branches that implement the compute plane. Those branches are not yet merged; the integrated tile is their union, landed by milestone #16 (Section 6). Dispositions: keep, new, rewrite, cherry-pick.

Version context: ISA 0.5.0 is the design target; the shipped SSOT (opcodes.py) is still 0.4.0 and the cutover (0.5.0 opcodes, PE_LATENCY 0 -> 2) is pending. See the ISA 0.5.0 status note.

docs.title: 01 Overview and current tree
docs.text:
The implementation maps the locked microarchitecture onto RTL modules. Module context (FIG. 1-2) is in the microarchitecture reference. The table below is the real npu/src/compute_tile/ tree with a disposition and source branch per file.

Locked · sequencer decomposition

The control front-end is pc.sv / pc_iram.sv / decoder.sv / dma_addr_gen.sv / perf_counters.sv (issues #204 / #180); the tile_sequencer.sv + loop_ctrl.sv / tile_agu_pkg.sv no-interlock FSM drives fetch / decode / issue on top of it (detail in Section 2).

docs.title: 02 Tile-sequencer implementation
docs.text:
The as-built tile_sequencer.sv + loop_ctrl.sv + tile_agu_pkg.sv (sequencer/feat-scaffold) implement the compute-tile control plane: fetch / decode / single-issue with no hardware interlock.

Fully-exposed: no hardware interlock

The sequencer issues one instruction per cycle and never stalls except on flush.slot / halt. There is no co-issue sync gate, no RAW hazard check, and no SYNC_MXU: the compiler owns all hazards via the reservation table (latency ledger in Section 6).

modulefiledispositionrolekey interfaceFSM Tile sequencer tile_sequencer.sv new (sequencer/feat-scaffold) fetch -> decode -> issue 1/cy, never stalls except flush.slot / halt; per-unit issue bus (stubbed until wired) per-unit issue / decode buses IDLE -> FETCH (3-cyc pipe) -> ISSUE, no sync gate PC + decode + IRAM pc.sv + decoder.sv + pc_iram.sv keep (reuse, structural) PC, IRAM dual-port, field decode, literal-pool redirect mux IRAM 4096 x 64b dual-port; decoded fields out - Loop control loop_ctrl.sv new (sequencer/feat-scaffold) LIFO loop-stack (depth 4) + iv counters + bo[8] base-offset accumulators + stride apply loop.begin reads {hi, stride vector} from the pool; loop.end does iv+=step / bo+=stride / branch / pop push / iterate / pop Per-unit AGU tile_agu_pkg.sv new (sequencer/feat-scaffold) combinational agu_add: eff = operand_literal + bo[channel], no multiplier; channels BO_CH_X=0, W=1, B=2, YS=3 literal + bo in; effective address out - DMU dmu.sv deferred (last, #16) async DMA, 2 slots (DMA_SLOT_COUNT=2); tile_sequencer keeps DMU external via flush.slot / halt drain (Section 7) descriptor in; dm_axi master; SPAD ports; slot_done[] out per-slot
  • Fetch depth: the FETCH pipe (F1 / F2 / F3, ~3 cycles) is the physical realization of the single architectural F stage (a pipelined IRAM BRAM read), not an extra architectural stage: it still issues 1 instruction/cycle in steady state, matching the F → D → X → W issue model in the microarch reference.
  • Literal pool: reuse the existing IRAM-embedded pool machinery rather than a separate LPM RAM; the pool loads with the kernel image and is read in Decode via the redirect mux (see the microarch LPM card).
  • DMA outstanding: 2 slots (hwparams DMA_SLOT_COUNT=2).
  • Validated: test_tile_sequencer.py (straight-line one-issue no-stall, flush.slot spin, halt drain, illegal-op safe-stop) and test_loop_ctrl.py (4-deep LIFO, bo[8] stride accumulation vs the 32x32 GELU bo-oracle). Unit dispatch is stubbed at this stage.

Three hand-authored Python SSOT sources drive five generated artifacts through one generator (npu/scripts/constgen.py). The generated .sv / .tcl carry a GENERATED - do not edit directly header; hand-edits revert on the next regen.

docs.title: 06 Integration #16 and latency ledger
docs.text:
Milestone #16 lands the union of the feature branches. It is not yet done. The integrated tile is rtl/feat-fp-pipeline (B, the pipelined fp core) with the row-pipelined vpu_ctrl, plus vreg_rf, lsu, tile_sequencer / loop_ctrl / tile_agu_pkg, and the X-from-VREG MXU datapath.

Integration steps

  • MXU reconciliation: integrate B's timing (SKEW=3) with mxu-vreg-x's X-source. Hand-port the X-from-VREG datapath into B's mxu.sv; do not git-merge mxu-vreg-x's mxu.sv or sequencer.sv.
  • Compute-plane top: extend compute_tile.sv to wire tile_sequencer dispatch to the units + vreg_rf
  • spad + an exposed ACC read port; AGU resolve per operand via tile_agu_pkg::agu_add.
  • VPU on vreg_rf: per DECISION 1, the VPU uses the R_vpu read port + the shared single write port (time-shared with LSU). Required for the worked-example e2e (acc.store -> gelu -> vreg.store all on V16).
  • LSU-SPAD RL=2: align to real MEM_LATENCY=2, generalize rd_data_q to an RL-deep shift register (LSU latency 9 -> 10), re-run test_lsu.
  • DMU internals (dmu.sv) deferred to last; tile_sequencer keeps the DMU external (Section 7).

docs.title: Reservation-table latency ledger
docs.text:
Compiler contract: a stale value is a miscompile. All figures are RTL / synth-measured and implementation-versioned (they move with the fp-pipeline pass).

quantityvaluenote PE_LATENCY2hwparams.py (0 -> 2); regenerated into npu_config_pkg.sv SKEW3PE_LATENCY + 1 matmul fill172N-1 + PE_LATENCY (N=8) D_vpu23VPU fixed pipeline depth (drain) exp25BRAM-LUT exp2 latency reduce tree93 * DEPTH LSU10ROWS + SPAD_RD_LATENCY at RL=2 (deployed); 9 at RL=1

Row-pipelining is PARTIAL

Only 5 single-source ACC ops stream at 1 row/cycle within a tile: scale.bcast, scale.col.bcast, scale.imm, sub.bcast, add.col.bcast. gelu / exp2 / div / two-source / rowsum / rowmax stay latency-serial (deeper or multi-read, not sim==synth-safe to stream yet). The speedup is within-tile only (about 5.9x per tile), not cross-tile sustained throughput. The external start / done / bram_active contract is unchanged.

docs.title: 07 DMU: semantics and ports
docs.text:
Deferred to last (#16)

DMU internals (burst sizing, FIFO depth, dm_axi error / timeout, arbitration) are deferred to the end of #16. Only the semantics and the port interface are fixed here; the tile_sequencer already keeps the DMU external via flush.slot / halt drain.

docs.title: Semantics
docs.text:
• Async DMA between devmem and SPAD; v1 2 slots (DMA_SLOT_COUNT=2).
  • dmu.load.spm = DRAM to SPAD; dmu.store.spm = SPAD to DRAM.
  • Descriptor = {SPAD row, devmem addr (literal-pool index; stride at pool[idx+1]), shape = (rows-1)<<8 | (cols-1)}.
  • On completion, sets a per-slot done bit that flush.slot waits on.
  • halt drains all outstanding transfers.

docs.title: mininpu Compute-Tile ISA Reference
docs.text:
mininpu · Doc 1 of 3 · Locked

Instruction Set Architecture reference for the mininpu matmul/vector accelerator compute tile, version 0.5.0.

ISA 0.5.0 · 15 opcodes · 64-bit word · in-order, no interlock

docs.title: Table of contents
docs.text:
1. Overview & Invariants
  2. State & Geometry
  3. Instruction Encoding
  4. Flags & Locked Decisions
  5. Timing & Reservation Contract
  6. Compiler Scheduling Contract
  7. Worked Example: 32x32 GELU
  8. Deferred / Notes

docs.title: 01 Overview and Invariants
docs.text:
A small, statically-scheduled instruction set of 15 opcodes for a matmul/vector accelerator tile.

  • Issue: in-order, single issue, 1 instruction/cycle.
  • NO producer-busy scoreboard, NO hardware interlock. The hardware never stalls a consumer for an in-flight producer and never detects a clobbered in-flight operand.
  • The compiler owns all hazard avoidance, latency padding, and structural serialization (see Section 6).
  • flush.slot is the only mid-program runtime synchronization primitive; halt is the terminal drain (end of kernel), not a mid-program sync.
  • Nonblocking set = {mxu.matmul, mxu.load.w, mxu.acc.init, async-DMA}; every other op is blocking.
  • Instruction word: 64-bit fixed width; operand storage is fixed per opcode, no runtime type tag.
  • dtype convention: bf16 in SPAD and DRAM (devmem); fp32 in VREG and ACC.
  • Launch: a kernel is a binary of NPU assembly; the command processor loads it into IRAM[0] and starts the sequencer, which runs to the terminal halt.

docs.title: 02 Architectural State and Geometry
docs.text:
Four explicitly-addressed data spaces (analogous to CUDA .global / .shared / .reg plus a tensor accumulator), plus MXU weight buffers and control state. No unified addressing: the space is implied by the opcode/operand slot.

Diagram deferred to the presentation refresh; see the text below for the current model.

space role addressed by geometry devmem off-tile DRAM (host-shared, global), bf16 byte address via literal-pool index + launch patch 256b words (DM_ADDR_WIDTH=27) SPAD on-chip scratchpad, bf16 (explicitly managed, NOT a cache) SPAD row index (10-bit), separate 0-based space 1024 x 256b = 32 KiB, 8 banks (addr % 8) VREG vector register file, fp32 VREG id (5-bit, 0..31) 32 x [8x8] fp32 = 8 KiB, 2D tile, whole-tile-row access (2r1w) ACC matmul accumulator, fp32 (MXU-owned) ACC buffer index; buffers {0,1} both used (depth-2 double-buffer) [8x8] fp32 x depth 2 = 512 B, 8 per-column banks wbuf weight buffer (MXU, stationary) implicit; mxu.load.w fills inactive, mxu.matmul promotes 2 (double buffer) control PC + induction vars - PC 12-bit; iv_reg x4 (LIFO nest depth 4), 32-bit each LPM literal pool: on-chip constant / param RAM in the tile sequencer (baked + launch-patched, read-only to the datapath) pool-index (8-bit, 0..255) 256 x 64b = ~2 KiB (provisional; final sizing during implementation); NOT devmem, NOT SPAD

docs.title: Datapath spine
docs.text:
stage detail outgoing edge devmem off-tile DRAM, bf16 DMU: async DRAM to SPAD copy. SPAD on-chip scratchpad, bf16 dtype boundary: vreg.load widens bf16 to fp32; vreg.store truncates fp32 to bf16. VREG register file, fp32 MXU streams X (cast fp32 to bf16 at the PE input); output lands in ACC. ACC accumulator, fp32 acc.store drains ACC to VREG, exact lossless fp32.
  • W path: SPAD to wbuf via mxu.load.w (weights stationary).
  • X path: VREG to PE array, streamed, cast fp32 to bf16 at the PE input.
  • Output path: PE array to ACC.
  • dtype boundary sits only at the SPAD-VREG edge.

docs.title: 2.1 Literal pool (LPM)
docs.text:
A fifth on-chip element, beside the four data spaces plus wbuf and control. It is the tile's constant store.

  • Address space: a separate pool-index space, 8-bit index (0..255); 256 x 64b entries (~2 KiB), provisional (final sizing during implementation); on-chip RAM inside the tile sequencer. NOT devmem, NOT SPAD.
  • Structure: an array of 64-bit constant entries. Entry classes: (a) loop descriptor = {hi bound, packed bo-stride vector}; (b) devmem base address (launch-patched); (c) scalar immediate.
  • Access: read-only to the datapath; read by the sequencer in Decode. Written once per launch = compiler-baked constants + command-processor launch patch (devmem bases via program_id/GRID_LINEAR + kernel_args). Part of the kernel image (loaded with the code).
  • Operands that reference it: loop.begin.op1 (pool index of the loop descriptor {hi, bo-stride vector}), dmu.*.op1 (devmem base; stride at pool[idx+1]), vpu.simd.op2 (immediate when bcast=imm).

docs.title: 2.2 Addressing model
docs.text:
• Effective address of a memory/register operand = operand_literal + bo[channel], where bo is a per-channel base-offset accumulator maintained by the sequencer. There is no address multiplier in the datapath: the per-unit AGU is just an adder.
  • bo is maintained by stride-accumulation over the loop nest: loop.begin pushes the current bo (LIFO) and loads the loop's stride vector from the pool into the loop frame; each loop.end iteration does bo[ch] += stride[ch]; loop exit pops.
  • So bo[ch] = sum over active loops of (iter * stride). The induction variable is the loop counter; the address math is the incremental accumulation (equivalent to const + sum(iv*stride) but multiplier-free).

docs.title: 03 Instruction Encoding
docs.text:
Single 64-bit word, fixed field layout.

 63        56 55      48 47        32 31        16 15         0
+-----------+----------+------------+------------+------------+
|  OP[7:0]  | FLAGS[7:0]|  op0[15:0] |  op1[15:0] |  op2[15:0] |
+-----------+----------+------------+------------+------------+
  • OP[63:56] - 8-bit opcode.
  • FLAGS[55:48] - 8-bit flags byte (per-op; unused bits reserved-must-be-zero).
  • op0[47:32] / op1[31:16] / op2[15:0] - three 16-bit operand slots, storage fixed per opcode.

docs.title: 3.1 Opcode table (SSOT)
docs.text: opcode mnemonic op0 op1 op2 flags writeback 0x00 mxu.load.w SPAD row (W tile) - - [0]=transB (reverse weight-row load order) wbuf (inactive) 0x01 mxu.matmul ACC (acc_out) VREG id (X, streamed) - [0]=accum, [1]=causal ACC 0x02 mxu.acc.init ACC (acc_out) SPAD row (bias, 1xN) - - ACC (bias broadcast to all rows) 0x10 vpu.reduce VREG dst VREG src - [0]=0 sum / 1 max VREG (per-row scalar to col0) 0x11 vpu.lut VREG dst VREG src - [1:0]=0 exp2 / 1 rsqrt / 2 gelu / 3 recip VREG 0x12 vpu.simd VREG dst VREG a VREG b (or pool-imm if bcast=imm) [4:2]=funct{add,sub,mul,max,fma,mov}, [1:0]=bcast{none,row,col,imm} VREG 0x30 acc.store VREG dst ACC src - - VREG (fp32 lossless) 0x32 vreg.load VREG dst SPAD row (src) - - VREG (bf16 to fp32 widen) 0x33 vreg.store SPAD row (dst) VREG src - - SPAD (fp32 to bf16 truncate) 0x40 dmu.load.spm SPAD row (dst) devmem addr (literal-pool index; stride at pool[idx+1]) shape = (rows-1)<<8 \| (cols-1) [4]=IMM_OP1 SPAD region 0x42 dmu.store.spm SPAD row (src) devmem addr (pool index) shape (0xFFFF = derive at lowering) [4]=IMM_OP1 DRAM 0x50 loop.begin iv id loop descriptor (pool index): (lo<<8) \| step, both 8-bit literals [4]=IMM_OP1 (immediate hi; no-bo loops only) push loop frame, IV=lo 0x51 loop.end iv id - - - IV += step; branch to body if IV < hi 0x53 flush.slot slot bitmask (16-bit immediate) - - - none (async-DMA region fence) 0x5F halt - - - - none (drain all DMU, then assert done)

docs.title: 3.2 Per-op semantics
docs.text:
• mxu.load.w - load W tile from SPAD[op0] into the inactive wbuf; transB reverses row load order; each mxu.matmul consumes exactly one preceding mxu.load.w.

  • mxu.matmul - streaming matmul into ACC[op0] from X = VREG[op1] and the active stationary weights (X cast fp32 to bf16 at the PE input):

    acc_out = (accum ? acc_out : 0) + X @ W^T

    causal masks the output upper triangle. A matmul with no pending mxu.load.w is illegal. - mxu.acc.init - seed ACC[op0] with a 1xN bias from SPAD[op1], broadcast to all rows. - vpu.reduce - per-row reduce of VREG[op1] to VREG[op0] col0; flag selects sum / max. - vpu.lut - elementwise unary exp2 / rsqrt / gelu / recip, VREG to VREG. - vpu.simd - elementwise ALU (add/sub/mul/max/fma/mov) with broadcast mode none/row/col/imm. - acc.store - drain ACC[op1] to VREG[op0], exact lossless fp32 (no conversion). - vreg.load - SPAD[op1] to VREG[op0], widen bf16 to fp32. - vreg.store - VREG[op1] to SPAD[op0], truncate fp32 to bf16. - dmu.load.spm - async DRAM to SPAD; async, 2 slots (DMA_SLOT_COUNT=2); region unsafe to consume until a matching flush.slot. - dmu.store.spm - async SPAD to DRAM; op2=0xFFFF derives shape at lowering. - loop.begin - push a loop frame on op0, set IV=lo (LIFO, nest depth 4). op1 is the pool index of the loop descriptor {hi bound, bo-stride vector} (always resident in the literal pool, so the stride vector is always available); the IMM_OP1 flag instead supplies an immediate hi and is used only for loops that drive no bo strides. - loop.end - IV += step; branch to body while IV < hi, else pop. - flush.slot - block until the masked DMU slot(s) report done; the only mid-program runtime sync (halt is the terminal drain, not a mid-program sync). - halt - drain all outstanding DMU, then assert done; ends the kernel.

docs.title: 04 Flags and Locked Decisions
docs.text:
Locked

flags[5] (ACC_TILE) is dropped from vpu.simd; the VPU is VREG-only. Bit 5 is reserved-must-be-zero on all opcodes.

Locked

ACC buffers {0,1} are both used for depth-2 double-buffering (acc_out / acc_src in [0, ACC_DEPTH=2)). The existing ACC buffer-index operand field is now plumbed to select the buffer (the depth index; no encoding change); an index >= ACC_DEPTH is still an illegal-op. This depth-2 buffer index is distinct from the 8 per-column banks (the physical column organization of the [8x8] accumulator).

Locked

VREG is a 2D [8x8] fp32 tile (5-bit id unchanged), not a flat vector.

Locked

DMU is async-only: the async-mode flag is retired; DMU flags[5] is reserved-must-be-zero.

docs.title: Reserved-must-be-zero
docs.text:
• All unused flag bits on every opcode.
  • flags[5] on all opcodes (dropped ACC_TILE).
  • DMU flags[5] (retired async-mode flag).

docs.title: 05 Timing and Reservation Contract
docs.text: Because there is no hardware interlock or scoreboard, the per-instruction cycle-accurate access windows are a normative compiler-hardware contract.

docs.title: 5.0 Notation
docs.text:
Single definition point for every symbol used across this document. Kind distinguishes ISA-visible structural constants from RTL / synth-measured implementation values (not locked) from reference/index symbols.

symbol meaning kind value / status t issue cycle of an instruction (reference point for all access windows) reference - N systolic tile dimension (rows/cols of the array; also the whole-tile-row width) STRUCTURAL, ISA-visible 8 (baseline) 2N-1 systolic array fill/drain span STRUCTURAL 15 at N=8 d matmul issue-to-first-X-read offset: matmul reads VREG[X] over [t+d, t+d+N) RTL / synth-measured TBD L_matmul matmul latency: issue to ACC written (ACC valid at t+L_matmul) RTL / synth-measured TBD R_mxu the MXU's VREG read PORT used to stream X (a port name, not a latency) port id - L_ldw mxu.load.w latency: issue to inactive wbuf loaded RTL / synth-measured ~N D_vpu VPU pipeline depth = per-row drain latency (result at t+D_vpu); row-pipelined for the single-source SIMD ops (1 tile-row/cycle), gelu / exp2 / two-source latency-serial (1 row / D_vpu) RTL / synth-measured, NOT a locked constant about 23 (measured after the fp-pipeline pass) PE_LATENCY (MXU PE pipeline depth) registered-MAC stages inside each PE (adds to the 2N-1 fill) RTL / synth-measured 2 (measured); matmul fill = 2N-1 + PE_LATENCY = 17 lo / hi / step loop lower bound / upper bound / increment (lo, step 8-bit immediate; hi from the literal-pool descriptor) index - iv loop induction variable (per active loop frame) index - bo[channel] per-channel base-offset accumulator; eff_addr = operand_literal + bo[channel] state 8 channels m, n, k output-row-tile / output-col-tile / contraction tile indices in the 4x4x4 grid index 0..3
  • The contract lives in the ISA but is implementation-versioned: the structure is fixed now; the absolute offsets are TBD (RTL-measured) and locked by the sim == synth requirement.
  • A kernel binary declares the timing-rev it was compiled against; a hardware timing-rev mismatch is rejected / flagged. This is the concrete form of "recompile if latencies change".
  • The concrete structural values are N and the systolic fill 2N-1. The VPU pipeline depth D_vpu, the MXU PE pipeline depth, the offsets (d, L_matmul), and all absolute numbers are RTL / synth-timing measured implementation values - D_vpu is currently about 23 (measured after the fp-pipeline pass), NOT an ISA-visible structural constant.
  • Throughput-bound, not latency-bound: these per-instruction latencies (including D_vpu and the matmul systolic fill) are negligible for performance - the tile is throughput-bound, so pipeline depth may grow to meet 200 MHz while throughput stays 1 tile-row/cycle (VPU) and 1 column/cycle (MXU). The VPU is row-pipelined for the single-source SIMD ops (scale.bcast, scale.col.bcast, scale.imm, sub.bcast, add.col.bcast): 1 tile-row issued per cycle, multiple rows in flight, and D_vpu is the per-row drain latency, amortized across the tile-loop. Row-pipelining is partial: gelu / exp2 and the two-source ops stay latency-serial (each row drains the full D_vpu before the next issues). The deep fp / reduce-tree pipelining (combinational fp_mul, registered IEEE fp_add) is intended design, not overhead.

Structural access windows (offsets symbolic; issue cycle = t):

instruction reads (port, window) writes (port, @cycle) notes mxu.matmul VREG[X] on R_mxu over [t+d, t+d+N) (1 column/cycle); wbuf(active) implicitly ACC at t + L_matmul the PE MAC is registered for 200 MHz with PE_LATENCY = 2 (measured), so fill = 2N-1 + PE_LATENCY = 17; VREG[X] is WAR-free only after t+d+N mxu.load.w SPAD over ~N cycles into wbuf(inactive) wbuf(inactive) double-buffered, so it overlaps the concurrent matmul streaming the other buffer mxu.acc.init SPAD bias row ACC (broadcast to all rows) - vpu.reduce / vpu.lut / vpu.simd VREG (x2 reads for simd, on the two whole-tile-row read ports) VREG on the single write port at t + D_vpu D_vpu = VPU pipeline depth, RTL / synth-measured (currently about 23, measured after the fp-pipeline pass); an implementation number, not a locked constant. Fixed and uniform across functions (gelu-set), so a result is available at issue + D_vpu acc.store ACC VREG on the single write port (LSU-driven) exact lossless fp32 vreg.load / vreg.store LSU: SPAD <-> VREG LSU: SPAD <-> VREG bf16 <-> fp32 cast at the boundary

VREG is 2r1w: the VPU writeback and the LSU writeback (acc.store / vreg.load) name the same single physical write port, time-shared by the compiler schedule so two writes never collide in a cycle. A 2nd write port is a sizing option only, not part of the baseline.

TBD · offsets RTL / synth-timing measured

The MXU PE pipeline depth is PE_LATENCY = 2 (measured), so the matmul fill = 2N-1 + PE_LATENCY = 17. d, L_matmul, the VPU pipeline depth D_vpu (currently about 23, measured after the fp-pipeline pass), and the remaining absolute numbers are TBD (RTL / synth-timing measured). Only N and 2N-1 are the ISA-visible concrete structural values. All latencies are FIXED and data-independent once measured, since the no-scoreboard model relies on it: simulation latency must equal synthesis latency - a wrong locked latency (a value that disagrees with the RTL-measured one) silently miscompiles.

docs.title: 06 Compiler Scheduling Contract
docs.text:
With no interlock and no scoreboard, program correctness depends entirely on the static schedule. The compiler MUST obey every rule.

  • RAW (latency-respecting placement): schedule a consumer no earlier than producer-issue + producer-latency; fill gaps with independent ops or NOPs.
  • WAR on inputs is unguarded: never overwrite a VREG / SPAD row / ACC buffer still being read by an in-flight instruction.
  • WAW ordering: two writes to the same destination must be program-ordered with non-overlapping write windows.
  • Structural hazards: one matmul at a time; exactly one mxu.load.w per matmul (single inactive wbuf); one VPU op-stream at a time.
  • VREG 2r1w (whole-tile-row): at most 2 reads + 1 write per cycle; keep the foreground unit's (VPU / LSU) VREG accesses in cycles disjoint from the MXU X-read burst (temporal separation, not banking).
  • DMU ordering: insert flush.slot before consuming a dmu.load.spm region; ensure producing vreg.stores complete before a dmu.store.spm reads; fence two async DMAs to the same region.
  • ACC discipline: seed (mxu.acc.init or accum=0), accumulate (accum=1, same buffer, in order), drain (acc.store, LSU-driven).
  • Loops: literal-only trip counts (lo/step 8-bit, hi from the pool); nest depth 4 (LIFO); no runtime-data-derived counts.
  • Addressing: compile-baked (literals + launch-patched pool indices + IV-relative base + iv*stride); no runtime data-dependent addressing in v1.
  • Recompilation: a binary is valid only for the latencies it was compiled against; recompile if any FU latency, N, or D_vpu changes.

docs.title: 07 Worked Example: 32x32 GELU(X @ W^T + b)
docs.text:
A full tile-loop example. Assume X already in VREG, and W and bias already in SPAD (DMU excluded). Geometry N=8, so a 32x32 matmul is a 4x4x4 tile grid (m,n,k in 0..3).

  • Layout: X[m][k] at VREG id 4m+k; Y scratch VREG16; W row = 16n+4k; bias row = n; Y row = 16m+4n.

docs.title: 7.0 Initial architectural state
docs.text:
What lives where before the tile-loop runs. Note the dtype boundary: bf16 in SPAD, fp32 in VREG and ACC (the cast sits at the SPAD-VREG edge).

space dtype / geometry contents before the loop VREG fp32, 32 x [8x8] X[m][k] tile at id 4m+k (ids 0..15); Y scratch at V16 - the compute working set SPAD bf16 W^T rows at row 16n+4k; bias rows at row n (base B_base) - the matmul operands LPM 64b entries the loop descriptors pool[0..2] + any pooled bases (see 7.0b) ACC fp32, MXU-owned, depth-2 empty; seeded per (m,n) block by acc.init, drained by acc.store

docs.title: 7.0b Literal pool contents
docs.text:
The concrete pool entries this kernel needs, so loop.begin op1 = pool[idx] (referenced in 7.2 as pool[2]={hi=4, k bo-strides}) is grounded. Stride values are taken from the 7.1 loop.end comments and match the 7.3 bo trace.

entry class contents pool[0] m-loop descriptor {hi=4; bo-stride: bo_X+=4, bo_Ys+=16} pool[1] n-loop descriptor {hi=4; bo_W+=16, bo_B+=1, bo_Ys+=4} pool[2] k-loop descriptor {hi=4; bo_X+=1, bo_W+=4} pool[3..] pooled bases (launch-patched) B_base, W_base, X_base, Y_base; the devmem base is patched at launch

docs.title: 7.1 Static program (body once, 12 static instructions)
docs.text:
loop.begin m (iv0,0,4,1)          ; push bo
  loop.begin n (iv1,0,4,1)        ; push bo
    acc.init  ACC0, B_base         ; bias row = B_base + bo_B
    loop.begin k (iv2,0,4,1)      ; push bo
      load.w  W_base               ; W row   = W_base + bo_W
      matmul  ACC0, X_base, accum  ; X id    = X_base + bo_X
    loop.end  k                    ; iter: bo_X+=1, bo_W+=4  | exit: pop
    acc.store V16, ACC0
    vpu.lut   V16, V16, gelu
    vreg.store Y_base, V16         ; Y row   = Y_base + bo_Ys
  loop.end  n                      ; iter: bo_W+=16, bo_B+=1, bo_Ys+=4 | exit: pop
loop.end  m                        ; iter: bo_X+=4, bo_Ys+=16 | exit: pop

docs.title: 7.2 64-bit encoding (example block m=1, n=2)
docs.text:
Word = OP[63:56] | FLAGS[55:48] | op0[47:32] | op1[31:16] | op2[15:0].

# asm OP FLAGS op0 op1 op2 effective addr 1 acc.init ACC0,B(n) 02 00 0000 0200 0000 bias = 0x200 + bo_B(2) = 0x202 2 loop.begin k 50 00 0000 0002 0001 op1=pool[2]={hi=4, k bo-strides}; op2 lo<<8\|step=0x0001 3 load.w W 00 00 0000 0000 0000 W row = 0 + bo_W(0x20) + k*4 4 matmul ACC0,X,accum 01 01 0000 0000 0000 X id = 0 + bo_X(4) + k = 4..7 5 loop.end k 51 00 0000 0000 0000 iv+=1, branch if k<4 6 acc.store V16,ACC0 30 00 0010 0000 0000 - 7 vpu.lut V16,V16,gelu 11 02 0010 0010 0000 - 8 vreg.store Y,V16 33 00 0300 0010 0000 Y row = 0x300 + bo_Ys(0x18) = 0x318

docs.title: 7.4 Cycle-level Gantt for one (m,n) block
docs.text:
Structural (N=8); the absolute L_* are implementation-versioned - FIG. 9 below uses the current RTL/synth-measured example latencies.

cy ->  0    2    4    6    8   10   12   14   16 ...(N=8 units)
SEQ  : Iai  Ilb  Ilw0 [nop/indep to cover L_ldw] Imm0 Ilw1 Imm1 Ilw2 Imm2 Ilw3 Imm3 Ile ... Ias Ilut Ivs
wbufA:      [ load W0 =N cy ][ stream mm0 =N cy ][ load W2 ][ stream mm2 ]
wbufB:                       [ load W1 =N cy  ][ stream mm1 ][ load W3 ][ stream mm3 ]
ACC  : [init]              +=mm0  +=mm1  +=mm2  +=mm3 -> drain(2N-1) -> acc.store -> gelu -> vreg.store

FIG. 9 - tile-loop pipeline Gantt for one (m,n) block: SEQ issue stream, weight double-buffer ping-pong (load.w ~ N=8 fully hidden), ACC init to += mm0..mm3 (each after fill = 2N-1 + PE_LATENCY = 17), then the LSU / VPU tail (acc.store to gelu D_vpu = 23 to vreg.store), with the ACC depth-2 overlap. open standalone

  • Steady state = 1 matmul / N=8 cy, load.w fully overlapped in the other weight buffer; absolute block time TBD; ACC depth-2 lets block j tail overlap block j+1 matmul.

docs.title: 08 Deferred / Notes
docs.text:
Status · 0.5.0 is the design target; SSOT cutover pending

ISA 0.5.0 is the locked design target. The shipped SSOT (mininpu/isa/opcodes.py) is still 0.4.0 / 38-opcode; the cutover to the 0.5.0 opcode set (incl. acc.init, vpu.lut function-select, vreg.store) plus encoders.py / ISS / npu_isa_pkg.sv regeneration and PE_LATENCY 0 -> 2 is PENDING (tracked separately; affects the compiler).

  • ACC buffer-index operand: now plumbed for depth-2 double-buffering (buffers {0,1}, the depth index, not the 8 per-column banks; no encoding change); a deeper ACC depth sweep (> 2) remains deferred (#313 / #262).
  • MXU addressing: drive path truncates to 13-bit (interim).
  • N: N=8 baseline; N is the systolic tile dimension, ISA-visible but sweepable without an encoding change (N=16 is an ISA-invariant sweep target: the same N realized as a wider 16x16 array, not an independent outer tiling factor).

End of mininpu Compute-Tile ISA Reference v0.5.0.

docs.title: Reference Microarchitecture
docs.text:
mininpu · Doc 2 of 3 · In progress

The datapath that implements ISA 0.5.0: fully-exposed 4-stage pipeline, per-unit AGU, MXU / VPU / VREG / ACC / SPAD, and the compiler-timed overlap model. Locked design plus the still-open decisions.

  • 1 instr/cycle, no scoreboard
  • pipeline F D X W
  • MXU 8x8 systolic
  • VPU 8 lanes
  • VREG 2r1w tiles

docs.title: Table of contents
docs.text:
1. Overview
  2. Execution model / pipeline
  3. Functional units & memories
  4. Overlap & roofline
  5. Decisions pending

This document describes the compute-tile microarchitecture behind ISA 0.5.0. Content is split into a Locked design and a Decisions pending section (still-open items).

Version context: ISA 0.5.0 is the design target; the shipped SSOT (opcodes.py) is still 0.4.0 and the cutover (0.5.0 opcodes, PE_LATENCY 0 -> 2) is pending. See the ISA 0.5.0 status note.

docs.title: 01 Overview
docs.text:
Top-level compute tile: the devmem-SPAD-VREG-ACC spine plus the MXU, VPU, and tile sequencer.

FIG. 1-2 - compute-tile datapath overview: the devmem to SPAD to VREG to ACC spine plus the MXU, VPU, and tile sequencer. open standalone

docs.title: Datapath spine
docs.text:
• devmem: off-tile DRAM (global)
    • DMU: async DRAM to SPAD.
  • SPAD: on-chip scratchpad, bf16
    • cast on vreg.load / vreg.store (bf16 to/from fp32).
  • VREG: vector register file, fp32
    • MXU streams X (cast fp32 to bf16 at PE); output to ACC.
  • ACC: matmul accumulator, fp32
    • acc.store drains ACC back to VREG (lossless fp32).

docs.title: Concurrency model
docs.text:
In-order issue, 1 instruction/cycle. No producer-busy scoreboard; no hardware interlock. All hazard avoidance is static compiler scheduling. flush.slot is the only mid-program runtime synchronization (halt is the terminal drain at end of kernel).

Nonblocking set = {mxu.matmul, mxu.load.w, mxu.acc.init, async-DMA}; everything else is blocking.

docs.title: Distributed per-unit AGU
docs.text:
Each unit has its own address-generation unit. The AGU is an adder: effective address = operand_literal + bo[channel], no multiplier.

operand_literal is a compile-baked immediate or pool index (devmem bases GRID_LINEAR launch-patched, not a CSR); bo is the per-channel base-offset broadcast from the tile sequencer.

docs.title: Base-offset (bo) addressing
docs.text:
8 base-offset channels in the sequencer; the AGU adds literal + bo with no multiplier.

bo is maintained by pool-sourced stride vectors: LIFO push/pop at loop.begin / loop exit, and bo[ch] += stride[ch] at each loop.end. Full rule: the ISA addressing model.

docs.title: Literal pool (LPM)
docs.text:
On-chip param RAM in the sequencer (256 x 64b (~2 KiB), provisional; final sizing during implementation), pool-index space, baked + launch-patched, read in Decode.

Realization: reuse the mature IRAM-embedded literal-pool machinery (loaded with the kernel image, read in Decode via the redirect mux), not a new separate RAM (see the implementation note).

Holds loop descriptors (hi + stride vector), devmem bases, and scalar immediates. See the ISA literal-pool section.

docs.title: 02 Execution model / pipeline
docs.text:
Locked - the fully-exposed, compiler-timed pipeline.

Performance model · throughput-bound, not latency-bound

The compute tile is throughput-bound. Per-instruction latency - including D_vpu and the matmul systolic fill - is negligible for performance: it is the pipeline drain latency, amortized across the tile-loop, not on a serial critical path. So pipeline depth may grow freely to meet timing while throughput stays 1 tile-row/cycle (VPU) and 1 column/cycle (MXU).

This rests on row-pipelining, which is partial. The single-source SIMD ops (scale.bcast, scale.col.bcast, scale.imm, sub.bcast, add.col.bcast) stream at 1 tile-row/cycle with multiple rows in flight (rows overlapped); for them D_vpu is only the drain latency of a single row, paid once and hidden behind the following rows, hence negligible. gelu / exp2 and the two-source ops stay latency-serial (each row drains the full D_vpu before the next issues). softmax / LayerNorm are dependent reduction chains within a row, but the tile-loop supplies the row-level parallelism the pipeline consumes, so for the streamable ops the pipe stays full.

The level-wise reduce-tree pipelining and the deep fp pipelines are intended design, not overhead: fp arithmetic follows standard pipelined designs (FloPoCo-style; fp_mul combinational, IEEE fp_add registered / multi-stage), so deepening a stage to close 200 MHz spends latency (which does not matter) to preserve throughput (which does).

  • F: fetch
    • fetch IRAM[PC], 1/cy.
  • D: decode
    • decode + per-unit AGU address gen + iv broadcast.
  • X: execute
    • op-specific latency.

  • W: writeback

    • to VREG / ACC / SPAD / DRAM per the op's writeback target.

  • Stages: F (fetch IRAM[PC], 1/cy) to D (decode + per-unit AGU address gen + iv broadcast) to X (execute, op-specific latency) to W (writeback to VREG / ACC / SPAD / DRAM per the op's writeback target). This is the architectural issue model; the physical Fetch is a pipelined multi-cycle IRAM BRAM read (~3 cycles) that still sustains 1 instruction/cycle issue in steady state (see the Doc 3 tile-sequencer FSM).

  • Fully exposed pipeline: the sequencer issues exactly 1 instruction per cycle and NEVER stalls to wait on a result. The only runtime waits are flush.slot (the sole mid-program sync, waits on an async-DMA slot) and halt (the terminal drain at end of kernel, not a mid-program sync).
  • Fixed, data-independent latencies: the compiler covers every latency by placing dependents later and filling gaps with independent instructions or NOPs. No hardware interlock, no scoreboard.
  • Nonblocking ops (mxu.matmul, mxu.load.w, mxu.acc.init, async-DMA) run in background FUs: the sequencer issues them in 1 cycle and proceeds. The compiler overlaps them (e.g. a background matmul concurrent with a foreground VPU op) and double-buffers the shared VREG tiles.
  • Completion: compute nonblocking ops are fixed-latency, so they need NO fence (compiler-timed). async-DMA is variable-latency, so the compiler inserts flush.slot before any consumer of the DMA'd region.
  • Structural single-instance limits: 1 MXU (matmuls serialize), 1 inactive weight buffer (at most 1 load.w preload ahead), 2 outstanding DMA slots (DMA_SLOT_COUNT=2; flush.slot's bitmask selects among them, async-only).
  • Latencies: the structurally-fixed values are N and the systolic fill = 2N-1; the VPU pipeline depth D_vpu, the registered-PE MAC / PE pipeline depth, and the absolute offsets (d, L_matmul) are RTL / synth-timing measured implementation numbers (D_vpu currently about 23 (measured after the fp-pipeline pass)), NOT ISA-visible structural constants.

FIG. 6 - tile sequencer: IRAM + folded PC/decoder, iv broadcast, loop-frame stack. open standalone

docs.title: 03 Functional units and memories
docs.text: Locked - unit geometry and roles.

docs.title: MXU
docs.text:
8x8 weight-stationary systolic array, bf16 x bf16 to fp32 accumulate, 2 weight buffers (active / inactive double-buffer).

X streams from VREG, cast fp32 to bf16 at the PE input (no separate X input buffer). Output to ACC. Weight-stationary dataflow.

PE MAC is registered for 200 MHz (PE latency to be confirmed by synth); systolic fill = 2N-1 plus the PE pipeline depth.

docs.title: VPU
docs.text:
Fixed-depth pipeline, 8 lanes, function-select {reduce | lut | simd}. Lane = column, row-serial: one 256-bit tile-row per cycle, 8 columns in parallel (the natural structure for the per-row reductions in softmax / LayerNorm). Throughput = 1 tile-row/cycle (row-pipelined, multiple rows in flight) for the single-source SIMD ops; gelu / exp2 / two-source ops are latency-serial (1 row / D_vpu).

Pipeline depth D_vpu is RTL / synth-measured (currently about 23 (measured after the fp-pipeline pass)), an implementation number, NOT an ISA-visible structural constant. fp_mul stays combinational (maps to a DSP), but an IEEE fp_add is registered (1+ pipeline stages, standard FloPoCo-style: a single combinational IEEE fp_add is ~11 ns and misses the 5 ns / 200 MHz budget); the reduce-sum tree is level-wise pipelined and each BRAM-read to compute cone is registered. D_vpu is therefore the sum of registered stages (RTL-measured), not an fp-op count. The deepest function (gelu) sets it; the other functions (reduce tree, exp2 BRAM, rsqrt, simd) align to the same depth so the reservation window is single. Reuses the fpu/* primitives (exp2 / rsqrt / tanh / recip BRAM LUTs, fp_mul / fp_add).

docs.title: VREG
docs.text:
32 x [8x8] fp32 = 8 KiB. Whole-tile-row access (256-bit), 2 read + 1 write ports (2r1w baseline).

No banking: MXU / VPU / LSU conflicts are avoided by temporal separation (foreground VREG access scheduled outside the short MXU X-read burst). See the realization subsection.

docs.title: ACC
docs.text:
[8x8] fp32 x depth 2 = 512 B. 8 per-column banks (the physical column organization: one accumulator column per systolic column). Separately, ACC buffers {0,1} are both used for depth-2 double-buffering; the ACC buffer field (the depth index) selects the buffer, not a column bank.

Depth-2 double-buffer: the second buffer overlaps a block's drain/store tail (acc.store → gelu → vreg.store) with the next block's matmul.

docs.title: SPAD
docs.text: bf16 storage. Separate, 0-based on-chip address space (like CUDA .shared). 32 KiB, 8 banks.

docs.title: LSU (load/store unit)
docs.text: A small dedicated load/store unit handling acc.store (ACC to VREG, fp32), vreg.load (SPAD to VREG, bf16 to fp32), and vreg.store (VREG to SPAD, fp32 to bf16).

docs.title: Loop control
docs.text:
loop.end branch bubble = 0: a counted loop with a precomputed target.

Full-unroll is infeasible (the unrolled GPT-2 static instruction count far exceeds the 4096-deep IRAM; a wider N=16 systolic array - the same systolic tile dimension N, a microarch sweep target, not an independent outer factor - gives about 8x fewer tiles than N=8), so outer tiling must be in-kernel nested loops driven by the base-offset (bo) accumulators (full rule: the ISA addressing model).

docs.title: Conflict model
docs.text:
No runtime interlock or scoreboard: the compiler statically avoids all VREG / SPAD port and bank conflicts using the reservation table (see the ISA timing contract); sim == synth is mandatory.

Optional debug-only conflict detector flags same-port / same-bank same-cycle access in debug builds (no stall): an optional bring-up aid, not part of the shipping datapath.

docs.text: FIG. 11 - shared fp primitives, stage/latency view: fp_add LATENCY=2 ({align+add} | {normalize} | {pack}, LUT/CARRY8) and fp_mul LATENCY=2 ({unpack} | {DSP48} | {round+pack}). The atoms every unit below is built from. open standalone

docs.title: MXU
docs.text:
FIG. 7 - MXU datapath: 8x8 weight-stationary systolic array, 2 weight buffers, X streamed from VREG (cast to bf16 at the PE input), output to ACC. open standalone

FIG. 12 - MXU matmul pipeline, stage/latency view: X burst-read (VREG) to fp32->bf16 cast to 8x8 systolic array (SKEW = PE_LATENCY+1 = 3, per-PE MAC = combinational fp_mul + registered fp_add L=2) to mxu_accumulator causal-fold RMW to ACC. Reservation offsets d(r) = 2N + 3r, PE_LAT = 5. Structural figure is FIG. 7, tile-loop Gantt FIG. 9. open standalone

docs.title: VPU pipeline
docs.text:
Locked structure - fixed-depth, partially row-pipelined vector pipeline (single-source SIMD ops stream; gelu / exp2 / two-source stay latency-serial); the depth D_vpu is RTL / synth-measured (currently about 23, measured after the fp-pipeline pass), not a locked constant.

  • Structure: 8 lanes (lane = column), row-serial at one 256-bit tile-row per cycle, throughput 1 tile-row/cycle (row-pipelined: multiple tile-rows in flight, 1 issued per cycle, each draining after D_vpu cycles) for the single-source SIMD ops only (scale.bcast, scale.col.bcast, scale.imm, sub.bcast, add.col.bcast); gelu / exp2 / two-source ops are latency-serial (1 row / D_vpu). fp_mul stays combinational (DSP), but an IEEE fp_add is registered (1+ pipeline stages, standard FloPoCo-style: a single combinational IEEE fp_add is ~11 ns and misses the 5 ns / 200 MHz budget); the reduce-sum tree is level-wise pipelined and each BRAM-read to compute cone is registered.
  • Depth is RTL-measured, set by gelu: D_vpu is the sum of the registered stages along the deepest function (gelu: fp_mul / registered fp_add chain + the tanh BRAM lookup), measured from RTL / synth (currently about 23, measured after the fp-pipeline pass), not a hand-counted fp-op total; the other functions (reduce tree, exp2 BRAM, rsqrt, simd) align to the same depth so the reservation window is single. D_vpu is an implementation number, in the same bucket as L_matmul and the PE pipeline depth, NOT an ISA-visible structural constant (unlike N and 2N-1).

FIG. 5 - VPU datapath: 8-lane fixed-depth pipeline (lane = column, row-serial), combinational fp_mul + registered IEEE fp_add, level-wise pipelined reduce tree. open standalone

FIG. 10 - VPU row-pipelining: latency-serial (8 x D_vpu = 184 cy) vs row-pipelined (~8 + 23 = 31 cy) schedules for an 8-row tile through the D_vpu = 23 pipeline; D_vpu is the per-row drain, amortized across the tile-loop. open standalone

FIG. 13 - VPU pipeline to the uniform D_vpu = 23 terminal, stage/latency view: per-function depths within the 23-cycle envelope (gelu ~22-stage sets it; exp2 = 5, reduce-tree = 9, single-src SIMD shallow, all padded to 23). The 5 single-source SIMD ops are row-pipelined; gelu / exp2 / two-source ops stay latency-serial. open standalone

docs.title: SPAD
docs.text: FIG. 8 - SPAD datapath: 32 KiB, 8 banks (bank = row mod 8), bf16; compute clients fixed-latency, the DMU yields on a bank conflict so compute never stalls. open standalone

docs.title: LSU pipeline
docs.text: FIG. 14 - LSU pipeline, stage/latency view: FIXED LATENCY = ROWS + SPAD_RD_LATENCY = 8 + 2 = 10; the three ops (acc.store, vreg.load, vreg.store) share one datapath, read tile-row r on cycle r, write RL=2 later, and own the bf16 ↔ fp32 casts. open standalone

docs.title: VREG realization (whole-tile-row RF + MXU-burst temporal separation)
docs.text:
Locked - resolved model.

  • Physical model: VREG = 32 registers, each an [8x8] fp32 tile (the paper design lifts the current 1D 8-wide vector RF to 2D tiles; hwparams VREG_SIZE 8 -> 64). Access granularity is a whole tile-row (256-bit).
  • Ports: 2 read + 1 write (whole tile-row), matching the whole-tile-row 2r1w RF. A 2nd write port is a sizing option only if two writes ever need to be concurrent; tail sub-ops are dependency-serial, so 2r1w is the baseline.
  • MXU X read: the MXU reads its X tile from VREG in a short burst (~N cycles) into its x_matrix staging buffer, then does not touch VREG for the rest of the matmul.
  • Conflict avoidance = temporal separation, not banking: the compiler schedules the foreground unit's (VPU / LSU) VREG accesses outside the MXU X-read burst window. Both windows are short, so this is easily schedulable.

Compiler rule

Keep the MXU X-read burst and any concurrent VPU / LSU VREG access in disjoint cycles. The MXU burst is a small fraction of the matmul, so the foreground unit has VREG to itself otherwise. Tail ordering is handled by the scheduler. The VPU has a single fixed pipeline depth (D_vpu, RTL / synth-measured, uniform across functions), so a VPU result is available at issue + D_vpu.

docs.title: MXU: reuse vs change vs the existing RTL
docs.text:
Locked - keep the trusted systolic core, evolve the memory interface toward the VREG tier.

aspect existing RTL paper design plan Systolic core + PE 8x8 weight-stationary array, bf16xbf16->fp32 MAC (pe.sv / systolic.sv) same reuse unchanged Weight source + double buffer SPAD -> 2 weight buffers (active/inactive), WPL concurrent preload same reuse Causal mask -inf fold at drain same reuse trans_b weight-row-reversal load same reuse X source SPAD VREG (read via R_mxu into the X staging buffer) change: re-source X from VREG fp32 -> bf16 cast on the SPAD read path cast at the MXU input stage change: move cast onto the VREG->staging path Matmul result stored back to SPAD stays in ACC change: remove store-back (drop S_STORE) ACC drain (acc.store) - result stays in ACC; the LSU drains ACC -> VREG fp32 change: add the drain, owned by the LSU (not the MXU) Bias init - acc.init = SPAD bias -> ACC broadcast change: add acc.init ACC buffering single depth-2 double-buffer, buffer field {0,1} (the depth index, not the 8 per-column banks) change: plumb the ACC buffer field
  • Reconcile: keep the full-tile X staging buffer (the existing RTL already has one). It shortens the R_mxu VREG occupancy to a ~N-cycle burst, which is what makes the temporal-separation VREG overlap (2r1w) conflict-free.
  • Timing note (corrected): matmul is staged, not a pure N-cycle stream; VREG reads are faster than the old SPAD path; absolute cycles are TBD (RTL, sim == synth). Keep 2N-1 as the weight-march / switch wavefront only.
  • File plan: pe.sv + systolic.sv unchanged; mxu.sv FSM re-sources X from VREG, drops S_STORE, adds the acc.init state + buffer wiring; mxu_accumulator.sv adds bias-init + buffer select (the matmul result stays in ACC); the LSU owns the ACC → VREG drain (acc.store); new cast on the X-load path.

docs.title: SPAD access model
docs.text:
Locked.

  • Geometry: 32 KiB = 1024 x 256b rows, 8 banks (bank = row mod 8), bf16 storage.
  • Clients: MXU load.w (weight read) and mxu.acc.init (bias read); LSU (vreg.load read, vreg.store write); DMU (devmem <-> SPAD, async). X is now sourced from VREG, so the MXU no longer streams X from SPAD. The VPU does not access SPAD directly - it operates on VREG; SPAD <-> VREG moves go through the LSU.
  • Fixed-latency contract: compute clients (MXU load.w / acc.init, LSU) get a fixed SPAD access latency; bank conflicts among compute clients are compiler-avoided (static scheduling, like VREG).
  • DMU absorbs the arbitration variability: on a bank conflict the DMU yields (it is async and flush-gated, so its latency is already variable / tolerated), so compute never stalls on SPAD. This reuses the existing banked_sram arbiter with priority compute (fixed) > DMU (yields).

Note

SPAD row access latency is RTL-derived (MEM_LATENCY=2 -> about 3 cycles per row) and enters the reservation table as a fixed value; the end-to-end reservation offsets remain TBD.

docs.title: 04 Overlap and roofline
docs.text:
Locked - qualitative bounds only.

  • MXU || VPU overlap: the compiler runs a background nonblocking op (e.g. a matmul) concurrent with a foreground blocking op (e.g. a VPU op) and double-buffers the shared VREG tiles.
  • MLP / matmul-heavy blocks are MXU-bound.
  • Tiny devmem matmuls are DRAM-BW-bound (bounded by the HP-port DRAM bandwidth, not on-chip compute).

FIG. 9 - tile-loop pipeline Gantt for one (m,n) block: weight double-buffer ping-pong (load.w fully hidden), ACC depth-2 tail overlap, steady state 1 matmul / N=8 cy. Canonical home: ISA 7.4. open standalone

Absolute cycle counts and roofline ratios are deliberately omitted here: they are latency-dependent and therefore TBD (RTL-measured) under the sim == synth contract. Only N and the systolic fill 2N-1 are structurally fixed; the VPU depth D_vpu is RTL-measured (currently about 23, measured after the fp-pipeline pass), like L_matmul and the PE pipeline depth.

docs.title: 05 Decisions pending
docs.text:
Still-open items.

Pending · reservation-window offsets

The reservation offsets (d, L_matmul, the MXU PE pipeline depth, the VPU pipeline depth D_vpu, and the absolute numbers in the ISA timing contract) are pending RTL / synth-timing and must be sim == synth verified. D_vpu is an implementation number (currently about 23 (measured after the fp-pipeline pass)), in the same bucket as L_matmul and the PE pipeline depth; only N and the systolic fill 2N-1 are the ISA-visible structurally-fixed values.

Pending · DMU internals

Descriptor slots, burst sizing, dm_axi error / timeout handling, and arbitration are deferred to implementation.

Pending · final sizing

LPM size and base-offset channel count final sizing. The VREG realization is now resolved (whole-tile-row RF + MXU-burst temporal separation; see the locked Functional units section). A 2nd VREG write port remains a sizing option only.
